title: Graph Transformers Get the GIST: Graph Invariant Structural Trait for Refined Graph Encoding

reviews.summary: The paper proposes GIST, a pairwise structural encoding for Graph Transformers that summarizes the common k-hop neighborhoods between node pairs as a k×k grid of exact-distance intersection cardinalities, augmented with fringe terms. To make this feasible, the authors estimate pairwise neighborhood intersections using MinHash for Jaccard similarities and HyperLogLog for union cardinalities, reducing complexity from O(k^2 n^4) to O(k^2 n^2). GIST is integrated as an attention bias and feature in a transformer-style block, and the method is evaluated on LRGB, ZINC/ZINC-full, and MoleculeNet, showing strong performance, including new SOTA on several ZINC benchmarks.
strengths: Introducing a k×k “substructure vector” that captures counts of nodes at exact k-hop distances from both endpoints is a clean, permutation-invariant way to encode pairwise higher-order structure beyond shortest-path distances or simple random-walk statistics.
Leveraging MinHash and HyperLogLog to build scalable, pairwise structural features extends sketching-based ideas from link prediction to global, pairwise features usable in Transformers.
Integrating GIST as a learnable bias in attention is a principled way to inject structural context at the core of the model rather than as purely positional encodings.
Broad set of datasets (LRGB peptides, ZINC and ZINC-full, and MoleculeNet) indicates generality across graph sizes and tasks (classification and regression).
Consistent improvements on ZINC and ZINC-full, with results surpassing strong baselines such as GRIT and Subgraphormer, support the practical value of the approach.
Ablation on k shows robustness and an interpretable trade-off.
The intuition behind GIST is well motivated with clear figures demonstrating how intersection patterns can disambiguate substructures.
High-level algorithmic flow is understandable, and the role of each component (MinHash, HLL, integration into attention) is explained.

Addressing structural encoding in Graph Transformers is an important and active research area; GIST offers a complementary route to SPD/LapPE/RWPE by focusing on pairwise intersection patterns of k-hop shells.
The empirical gains on popular molecular datasets suggest immediate utility for the community working on graph transformers for chemistry and biology.
weaknesses: The product of two unbiased estimators (MinHash-based Jaccard and HLL-based union) is not necessarily unbiased without independence and/or additional assumptions; the paper asserts unbiasedness without a careful justification of independence or bias correction.

The theorem and associated proof use “invariance” language where “permutation equivariance” is expected for node-wise mappings; the presentation suggests ψ(x_u) is invariant under isomorphism, which, if taken literally, would make the node mapping constant. This needs to be fixed precisely.
The definition of T terms and some recurrences have notational inconsistencies (e.g., T_{k_u-1,k}(u) vs T_{k_u-1,k}(u,v)) and require more careful derivation to ensure correctness.

Lack of runtime/memory profiling and scalability discussion. GIST stores an n×n×(k^2+2k) tensor per graph; for larger graphs this can be prohibitive. There is no empirical analysis of compute/memory overhead vs. baselines or a study of sketch sizes (m, p) vs. accuracy/time.

The attention mechanism deviates from standard softmax normalization (using a sigmoid on a dot with e_{u,v}) without justification or ablation vs. standard multi-head scaled dot-product attention with bias; this complicates attribution of gains to GIST vs. architectural choices.

MoleculeNet evaluation details are insufficient: missing clarity on splits (scaffold vs. random), input features/preprocessing, and tuning budget parity across baselines; this affects fairness and comparability.
No controlled comparison where GIST replaces an existing structural bias in a matched Graphormer/GPS/GRIT implementation to isolate the effect of the proposed encoding.
Several notational slips and typos in definitions and proofs; some equations are ambiguous enough to impede precise reimplementation (especially for T terms).
The proof sketch in Appendix A is informal and contains problematic equalities of sets under isomorphism; it should present a rigorous permutation-equivariance argument for the model outputs.

Prior pairwise structural encodings relevant to subgraph-level discrimination are under-discussed, e.g., DRNL/SEAL-style labels, distance encodings, and other pairwise encodings used in link prediction and subgraph GNNs.
A deeper comparison to Graphormer’s edge/shortest-path/centrality encodings and to GPS/GRIT’s structural biases would help position GIST more precisely.
reviews.questions:
1. Is the product estimator of intersection size truly unbiased in your implementation? Are the MinHash and HLL estimators independent (using independent hash families), and do you apply bias correction for HLL? Please provide a derivation or an empirical calibration (exact vs. estimated) with bias/variance plots.
2. Why depart from standard softmax attention? Could you report ablations comparing (a) standard softmax attention with additive GIST bias vs. (b) your sigmoid gating vs. (c) bias-only integration? This would help isolate the benefit of GIST vs. architectural choices.
3. Please clarify the exact definition and computation of the T terms and fix the notational inconsistencies (e.g., T_{k_u-1,k}(u) vs. T_{k_u-1,k}(u,v)). A step-by-step pseudo-code for constructing S_k(u,v) from C_{k_u,k_v} would aid reproducibility.
4. What are the MoleculeNet split protocols (scaffold vs. random), and what input features are used? How do you ensure comparable hyperparameter tuning and training budgets vs. baselines?
5. Can you provide runtime and peak memory comparisons against GRIT/Graphormer/GPS on LRGB/ZINC, as well as sensitivity to sketch sizes (m, p)? How does training time scale with n and k in practice?
6. Does GIST preserve performance under sparse attention (e.g., top-K or locality-restricted) to reduce O(n^2) costs? Any preliminary results?
7. Could you formalize the model’s permutation equivariance property with a proper statement and proof (and correct Theorem A.1)? Also, can you characterize the expressivity of GIST: are there graph pairs or substructures that GIST can distinguish but SPD/RWPE cannot?
reviews.assessment: This paper addresses an important open question—how to inject rich, general-purpose structural bias into Graph Transformers—and proposes a compelling pairwise encoding based on exact-distance intersection cardinalities, along with practical sketching to make it computationally feasible. The idea is intuitive, well-motivated, and empirically promising, with notable gains on ZINC/ZINC-full and competitive results elsewhere. That said, there are several issues that should be addressed for ICML: the estimator unbiasedness claim needs justification or correction; the theoretical section should be reframed around permutation equivariance with a rigorous proof; clarity issues in definitions (especially T terms) should be fixed; and more thorough experimental analyses (runtime/memory, attention formulation ablations, fairness on MoleculeNet) are necessary. With these improvements, the contribution would be significantly strengthened. In its current form, I view the paper as promising and of interest to the graph learning community, but recommend revisions to solidify technical correctness and empirical rigor.
